title: ds-viz-table - option matrix (light)
description: Test bench for splunk.table. Walks every documented option group: chrome (count, font, fontSize, headerVisibility, showRowNumbers, showInternalFields, backgroundColor), tableFormat (align, cellTypes incl. SparklineCell, rowBackgroundColors, rowColors, headerBackgroundColor, headerColor, sparklineColors, sparklineAreaColors, sparklineTypes), and columnFormat (per-column align, cellTypes, width, rowBackgroundColors, rowColors, sparklineColors). Includes heatmap row-shading via _color_rank rank field + seriesByName + rangeValue.

options.query: | makeresults count=5 | streamstats count AS rn | eval host = case(rn==1,"web-01",rn==2,"web-02",rn==3,"db-01",rn==4,"app-03",1==1,"cache-02") | eval severity = case(rn==1,"critical",rn==2,"high",rn==3,"medium",rn==4,"low",1==1,"info") | eval cpu = case(rn==1,94,rn==2,82,rn==3,71,rn==4,55,1==1,33) | eval message = case(rn==1,"Disk full",rn==2,"High latency",rn==3,"Slow query",rn==4,"Job retry",1==1,"Healthy") | table host severity cpu message

| makeresults count=5 | streamstats count AS rn | eval host = "web-" . tostring(rn) | eval status = case(rn<=2,"ok",1==1,"degraded") | eval _internal_id = "id-" . tostring(rn*100) | table _time host status _internal_id

ds_heatmap.name: heatmap - severity rank for row tinting

options.query: | makeresults count=5 | streamstats count AS rn | eval service = case(rn==1,"checkout",rn==2,"login",rn==3,"search",rn==4,"recs",1==1,"static") | eval severity = case(rn==1,"critical",rn==2,"high",rn==3,"medium",rn==4,"low",1==1,"info") | eval errors_per_min = case(rn==1,142,rn==2,67,rn==3,28,rn==4,9,1==1,1) | eval _color_rank = case(severity=="critical",1,severity=="high",2,severity=="medium",3,severity=="low",4,1==1,5) | table service severity errors_per_min _color_rank

ds_sparkline.name: sparkline - hosts with cpu mem req err trends

options.query: | makeresults count=288 | streamstats count AS rn | eval _time = relative_time(now(), "-" . tostring((288-rn)*5) . "m") | eval host = case((rn-1) % 4 == 0, "web-01", (rn-1) % 4 == 1, "web-02", (rn-1) % 4 == 2, "db-01", 1==1, "app-03") | eval base_cpu = case(host=="web-01",75,host=="web-02",55,host=="db-01",45,1==1,28) | eval base_mem = case(host=="web-01",68,host=="web-02",50,host=="db-01",72,1==1,32) | eval base_req = case(host=="web-01",1400,host=="web-02",640,host=="db-01",240,1==1,80) | eval base_err = case(host=="web-01",18,host=="web-02",4,host=="db-01",2,1==1,1) | eval cpu = base_cpu + (rn % 25) - 12 | eval mem = base_mem + (rn % 18) - 8 | eval req = base_req + (rn % 80) * 4 - 160 | eval err = base_err + (rn % 7) - 3 | stats latest(cpu) AS current_cpu, sparkline(avg(cpu), 30m) AS trend_cpu, sparkline(avg(mem), 30m) AS trend_mem, sparkline(sum(req), 30m) AS trend_req, sparkline(sum(err), 30m) AS trend_err by host

ds_align.name: align - mixed types for alignment demo

options.query: | makeresults count=4 | streamstats count AS rn | eval product = case(rn==1,"Headphones",rn==2,"Speaker",rn==3,"Mic",1==1,"Cable") | eval orders = case(rn==1,1284,rn==2,672,rn==3,341,1==1,89) | eval revenue_usd = case(rn==1,38520.50,rn==2,16800.00,rn==3,8525.25,1==1,1335.10) | eval status = case(rn==1,"OK",rn==2,"OK",rn==3,"WARN",1==1,"FAIL") | table product orders revenue_usd status

ds_format_numbers.name: format - regional revenue for number formatting demo

options.query: | makeresults count=5 | streamstats count AS rn | eval region = case(rn==1,"NA",rn==2,"EMEA",rn==3,"APAC",rn==4,"LATAM",1==1,"AFR") | eval orders = case(rn==1,128450,rn==2,67230,rn==3,89120,rn==4,12340,1==1,4520) | eval revenue_usd = case(rn==1,3852050.50,rn==2,1680000.25,rn==3,2245125.75,rn==4,310925.00,1==1,98775.40) | eval conversion_pct = case(rn==1,7.4,rn==2,4.2,rn==3,5.8,rn==4,2.1,1==1,1.4) | table region orders revenue_usd conversion_pct

ds_inventory.name: inventory - hidden field drives coloring of product

options.query: | makeresults count=4 | streamstats count AS rn | eval product = case(rn==1,"Headphones",rn==2,"Speaker",rn==3,"Mic",1==1,"Cable") | eval _inventory = case(rn==1,45,rn==2,12,rn==3,0,1==1,8) | eval price_usd = case(rn==1,89.99,rn==2,149.99,rn==3,55.50,1==1,9.99) | table product _inventory price_usd

options.markdown:
# `splunk.table` - option matrix

The go-to viz for showing rows and columns. Comes with three layered formatting tiers:

1. **Global chrome** in `options` (font, headers, count, backgroundColor)
2. **`tableFormat`** for whole-table effects (align grid, cell renderers, row tinting, header colours, sparklines)
3. **`columnFormat`** for per-column overrides (width, align, cell type, rowBackgroundColors of one column)

Dynamic options live in `context` and are referenced via DOS expressions: `> table | seriesByName('rank_field') | rangeValue(bands)`.

**Always set `showInternalFields: false`** to hide rank fields like `_color_rank` from the rendered output.

viz_default.title: 1. Default - all 5 rows, header inline, internal fields shown
viz_default.description: Pure default. count=10 (we have 5 rows so all show), headerVisibility=inline, showRowNumbers=false, showInternalFields=true. Columns auto-aligned by type.

viz_count_pagination.title: 2. count: 3 + headerVisibility: fixed + showRowNumbers: true
viz_count_pagination.description: count caps the rendered rows (paginator handles the rest). headerVisibility=fixed pins the header so it stays put when scrolling. showRowNumbers prepends a row index.

viz_internal_hidden.title: 3. showInternalFields: false (hides _internal_id, but _time stays)
viz_internal_hidden.description: Doc note: 'The _time field will still be shown if false.' Underscore-prefixed user fields like `_internal_id` and our `_color_rank` rank field are hidden, but `_time` is exempt. ALWAYS set this to false when you use a `_color_rank` pattern.

viz_font_mono.title: 4. font: monospace + fontSize: small
viz_font_mono.description: Monospace for log/payload tables where columns line up vertically. fontSize: extraSmall (10px), small (12px), default (14px), large (16px).

viz_align_columnformat.title: 5. columnFormat: per-column align + width
viz_align_columnformat.description: Set alignment and width per column. Numbers right-align, status center, text left. width is in pixels. The `align` value here is a single string (the column applies it to every cell) - the GLOBAL `tableFormat.align` is a 2D array.

viz_header_colors.title: 6. tableFormat.headerBackgroundColor + headerColor
viz_header_colors.description: Header chrome via tableFormat. Global static colours - the value is a literal hex, not a DOS expression. Use this to match the panel chrome to a brand colour or to deemphasise the header.

viz_heatmap.title: 7. Heatmap rows via _color_rank + rangeValue (severity tinting)
viz_heatmap.description: The classic pattern. Compute a numeric rank field upstream (`_color_rank`), keep `showInternalFields: false`, and feed the rank into `tableFormat.rowBackgroundColors` and `rowColors` via DOS. The bands in `context` map rank to colour. Notice critical row reads RED end-to-end without explicit row-by-row config. Header colours are intentionally NOT overridden here so they fall back to `themes.defaultHeaderBackgroundColor` and stay readable in both dark and light theme.

options.markdown:
## 8. Sparkline coloring — the four canonical patterns

Four distinct distribution semantics, all driven by **DOS expressions**. **Static `["#hex"]` arrays do not work** for `sparklineColors` — the renderer ignores them past row 0. Always use a DOS expression in `context`.

| # | Where | Distribution semantic | Use when |
|---|---|---|---|
| **8a** | `tableFormat.sparklineColors` + DOS `pick(<contextArray>)` | Per row, by row index. Row 0 → colour 0, row 1 → colour 1, recycles. | Colour should track row identity (host, region) |
| **8b** | `columnFormat.<col>.sparklineColors` + DOS `seriesByName(driver) | rangeValue(bands)` | Per row, threshold-driven, **scoped to one column**. | Each column maps a different metric, each row's colour reflects severity for that metric |
| **8c** | `tableFormat.sparklineColors` + DOS `seriesByName(driver) | rangeValue(bands)` | Per row, threshold-driven, **across all sparkline columns in the row**. | Whole-row severity colour (one driver column shades every sparkline in that row) |
| **8d** | `tableFormat.sparklineColors` + DOS `pick(staticColor)` with 1-element context | Uniform. All sparklines paint the same colour. | You just want one consistent colour everywhere |

All four examples below use the same `ds_sparkline` dataset (4 hosts × 4 trend columns) so you can compare visually.

viz_8a_dos_pick.title: 8a. tableFormat.sparklineColors via DOS pick() — per-row by index
viz_8a_dos_pick.description: Per-row colour distribution. `> table | pick(trendSparklineColors)` walks the 1D context array by row index: row 0 → colour 0, row 1 → colour 1, … When the array is shorter than the row count, the renderer recycles back to colour 0. Within one row, all four sparkline columns share the same colour. Use when colour should track row identity.

viz_8b_columnformat_dos.title: 8b. columnFormat.<col>.sparklineColors via DOS rangeValue() — per-row, threshold-driven, scoped to one column
viz_8b_columnformat_dos.description: **The canonical per-column pattern.** Each `columnFormat.<col>.sparklineColors` takes a DOS expression `> table | seriesByName('<driver>') | rangeValue(<bands>)`. The renderer evaluates the pipeline once per row, producing a per-row colour that lives **only inside that one column**. Here `current_cpu` drives `trend_cpu` (CPU bands), and `trend_req` is driven by `current_cpu` too but with different bands so the same metric can encode different scales per column. Use when each column maps a distinct metric and you want each row's colour to reflect that column's threshold. **Static array shapes (`["#hex"]` or `[["#hex"]]`) silently fail — only DOS expressions work here.**

viz_8c_tableformat_rangevalue.title: 8c. tableFormat.sparklineColors via DOS rangeValue() — per-row, threshold-driven, all columns
viz_8c_tableformat_rangevalue.description: Same DOS expression as 8b, but applied at `tableFormat` level so the colour propagates across every sparkline in that row. Each host's four sparklines all turn the same colour based on the host's `current_cpu` band: green (<50), yellow (50-70), orange (70-90), red (>=90). Use when colour should encode whole-row severity rather than per-metric thresholds.

viz_8d_uniform.title: 8d. tableFormat.sparklineColors via DOS pick(static) — uniform colour everywhere
viz_8d_uniform.description: Simplest of the four. A 1-element context array fed through `pick(staticColor)` broadcasts the single colour across all rows and all sparkline columns. Use when you want a consistent brand colour for every sparkline in the table without thresholds or per-row variation.

options.markdown:
## 9. Number formatting and field-driven colouring

Three more patterns from the [10.2 Table docs](https://help.splunk.com/en/splunk-enterprise/create-dashboards-and-reports/dashboard-studio/10.2/visualizations/table) Examples section:

- **9a - Number formatting** via `columnFormat.<col>.data` + `formatByType(...)`. Adds thousand separators, currency prefix, percent suffix.
- **9b - Per-column heatmap** via `columnFormat.<col>.rowBackgroundColors`. Tints **only that column's cells** by value, leaving other columns alone.
- **9c - Hidden field drives coloring**. `_inventory` is hidden via `showInternalFields: false`, but its values still drive `rowBackgroundColors` on the visible `product` column.

viz_9a_number_format.title: 9a. columnFormat.data + formatByType - number formatting
viz_9a_number_format.description: Numbers get thousand separators (`128,450`), currency gets `$` prefix (`$3,852,050.50`), percent gets `%` suffix (`7.4%`). Each column declares a `data: "> table | seriesByName('col') | formatByType(<contextKey>)"` and the corresponding context entry sets `{ "number": { "thousandSeparated": true, "unitPosition": "after" } }` plus prefix/suffix unit. This is the **canonical** way to pretty-print numbers in tables.

viz_9b_per_column_heatmap.title: 9b. columnFormat.<col>.rowBackgroundColors - per-column heatmap
viz_9b_per_column_heatmap.description: Heatmap that lives **inside one column only**. `columnFormat.orders.rowBackgroundColors` paints just the `orders` cell per row using a `rangeValue()` band on `orders` itself, while every other cell stays default. Compare to panel 7 where `tableFormat.rowBackgroundColors` tints the entire row. Use this when one metric is the anchor and you don't want a full-row tint dominating the rest of the grid.

viz_9c_hidden_field_driver.title: 9c. Hidden field drives coloring - _inventory → product cell colour
viz_9c_hidden_field_driver.description: `_inventory` is hidden by `showInternalFields: false`, but its numeric value still drives the `rowBackgroundColors` on the visible `product` column. Out-of-stock (`_inventory==0`) shows the product name on red; low stock (`<20`) on amber; healthy (`>=20`) on green. Same pattern as the official Splunk "Hidden field to color another field" example. The same trick works for any underscore-prefixed field; common names are `_color_rank`, `_color_hex`, `_status_code`.

viz_count_one.title: 10. count: 1 + headerVisibility: none
viz_count_one.description: Stripped down. headerVisibility=none kills the header row entirely - useful for KPI mini-tables embedded in a grid where the title carries the meaning.

viz_bg_color.title: 11. backgroundColor: panel tint
viz_bg_color.description: Global backgroundColor at the table level. Different from the panel chrome - this paints the cells. Pair with header colours so the gradient reads as one unit.

viz_compact_large.title: 12. fontSize: large + headerVisibility: fixed
viz_compact_large.description: Bigger font (16px) for an executive readout. Combined with fixed header for scroll friendliness even though count<rows.

viz_align_grid.title: 13. tableFormat.align via DOS - auto by type
viz_align_grid.description: Default global align: `> table | type() | matchValue(tableAlignByType)`. Numbers go right, strings left, mixed center. For full control, override per-column via columnFormat.align (1D array) or the global tableFormat.align (2D array).
